summary: Pure internal refactor of the pricing calculator into smaller, better-named functions. Behavior must be preserved; existing unit tests are the parity proof and reviewer must confirm no behavior change.

reversibility.why: Refactor is reverse-able via git; no state or contract changes to unwind.

blast_radius.why: Pricing calc is money-adjacent — small drift becomes financial error, which warrants test rigor even on an internal refactor.

compliance_scope.why: No regulated data surface; pricing logic is business logic but not in a compliance framework here.

user_facing_impact.why: Intended to be invisible; users should see no change.

scope_effort.why: Contained to the calculator module and its direct callers.

state_complexity.why: No persistence or cache semantics change.

operational_risk.why: No runtime behavior intended to change; risk is behavioral drift, not ops.

specialists.name: senior-engineer
specialists.why: Drive the decomposition shape and mentor reviewers on parity checks.

specialists.name: backend-engineer
specialists.why: Execute the refactor and keep test green.

specialists.name: test-designer
specialists.why: Verify existing coverage exercises the refactor and add characterization tests where gaps exist.

phases.name: design
phases.why: Decide decomposition boundaries before cutting functions apart.

phases.name: test
phases.why: Run existing tests + add characterization tests to prove parity.

phases.name: review
phases.why: Senior engineer confirms behavior parity and improved readability.

rigor_why: Money-adjacent logic earns enforced unit-test gate even without external amplifiers; minimal would be too loose for pricing.
complexity_why: Small, self-contained module with a single concern (decomposition).

tasks.title: Design decomposition boundaries for pricing calculator
Execute refactor: extract smaller, better-named functions
Run existing suite + add characterization tests as needed
